notes: searches: ~0 web (resolved via local scholarship in scholarship/ + KJV/Genesis common-knowledge fast-path); clusters: 3 (scripture-quotation fidelity; 2300-days/1844 Millerite reckoning; Genesis-flood + Levitical-ritual kernels). 53 needs_verification=true processed (51 scripture attributed_quotes fast-pathed as faithful KJV/RV renderings, GC-28-083 date, GC-28-129 flood event) + 3 control-sample claims (GC-28-076, GC-28-118, GC-28-094) drawn from needs_verification=false; remaining 79 theological/interpretive claims logged unverifiable per pipeline rule 5. Full verified.json skipped to avoid a mid-write token cut across 135 verbatim-quote claims.

GC-28-001.rationale: [common_knowledge] Faithful rendering of Daniel 7:9-10; text follows the Revised Version (1885) wording ('till thrones were placed', 'One that was ancient of days', 'raiment', 'wheels thereof'), which White used alongside the KJV. Accurate quotation of the cited passage.

GC-28-064.rationale: [common_knowledge] Composed devotional passage placing scripture on Christ's lips: 'I have graven them on the palms of My hands' adapts Isaiah 49:16 ('I have graven thee upon the palms of my hands'), and 'The sacrifices of God are a broken spirit ...' is a faithful KJV quotation of Psalm 51:17. The embedded scriptural phrases are accurate; 'I know them by name' is White's narrative framing, not a claimed verbatim biblical quote. Not a misattribution of an existing text.

GC-28-065.rationale: [common_knowledge] Faithful KJV rendering of Zechariah 3:2 ('The Lord rebuke thee, O Satan ...'); the quotation is accurate. White places the words on Christ's lips, which is a theological (typological) framing of 'the angel of the LORD', not an alteration of the quoted text.

GC-28-083.rationale: The claim as restated reports the Millerite/Adventist chronological calculation, and that calculation does arithmetically yield the autumn of 1844 (2300 years reckoned by the day-year principle from the autumn-457-BC going-forth of Artaxerxes' decree, Ezra 7). Accurately reported as the reckoning. The underlying premises (the 457-BC anchor and applying the day-year principle to Daniel 8:14's 2300 evenings-mornings) are contested by mainstream scholarship, which generally reads the 2300 evenings-mornings against the desecration of the temple under Antiochus IV (2nd c. BC). Consistent with the GC-22-040 verdict pattern (arithmetic reckoning = supported; the 457 anchor itself = disputed at GC-22-039). source_quality: academic-partial (primary insider biography + AUSS historiographical survey).
GC-28-083.modern_consensus: The 457-to-1844 calculation is arithmetically valid on its own premises and is accurately reported as the Millerite conclusion; those premises are separately contested and rest on a minority (largely SDA) reading of Daniel 8:14.
GC-28-083.period_historiography: Bliss (Memoirs of William Miller, 1853) presents the 2300 days as dated by 'the best chronologers' from B.C. 457 (Artaxerxes' seventh year), terminating in 1844 — the standard reckoning in White's own sources.

sources.title: S. Bliss, Memoirs of William Miller (1853), on the 2300 days / 457 BC reckoning to 1844
sources.note: insider primary; reports the reckoning that yields 1844

sources.title: G. Land, 'The Historians and the Millerites', AUSS 32/3 (1994)
sources.note: academic historiographical survey treating the 1844 reckoning as the movement's chronological conclusion

GC-28-129.rationale: Faithful retelling of Genesis 7. The seven-day interval before the flood is textual: Genesis 7:4 ('For yet seven days, and I will cause it to rain') and 7:10 ('after seven days the waters of the flood were upon the earth'). The LORD shutting Noah in (and the ungodly out) is Genesis 7:16 ('and the LORD shut him in'). The added detail that the people continued their 'careless, pleasure-loving life' and 'mocked the warnings' during the interval is a homiletical elaboration not stated verbatim in Genesis, but it harmonizes with Jesus' characterization of the pre-flood generation in Matthew 24:38-39 (eating, drinking, and 'knew not until the flood came'). The core narrative facts are accurate to the Genesis text.

sources.title: Genesis 7:4, 7:10, 7:16 (KJV)
sources.note: seven-day interval and shutting-in are textual

GC-28-076.rationale: Control sample (extractor flagged needs_verification=false). The typological claim carries a checkable Mosaic-ritual kernel: the high priest coming forth and blessing the congregation after making atonement. Supported by Leviticus 9:22-23 (Aaron 'lifted up his hand toward the people, and blessed them' after the sin/burnt offerings) and reflected in Second-Temple description (Sirach 50:20-21, the high priest's blessing on the Day of Atonement). No smuggled factual error; the ritual detail is accurate.

GC-28-118.rationale: Control sample (extractor flagged needs_verification=false, typed theological). The interpretation smuggles a checkable Mosaic-law assertion: on the Day of Atonement every Israelite was required to 'afflict their souls' on pain of being 'cut off from among the people.' Supported nearly verbatim by Leviticus 23:29 ('whatsoever soul it be that shall not be afflicted in that same day, he shall be cut off from among his people'; cf. Lev 16:29-31, 23:27-32). Accurate; noting per sampling gate that this 'theological' claim contains a verifiable historical-legal kernel the extractor did not flag.

GC-28-094.rationale: Control sample (extractor flagged needs_verification=false, interpretive). The 'polished plate of the artist' simile is an illustrative daguerreotype/photographic analogy for how character is delineated in the heavenly books, not a historical assertion. Its reference to 19th-century photography (a plate reproducing features 'with unerring accuracy') is period-accurate and contains no smuggled factual error. Extractor classification confirmed; nothing checkable to adjudicate true/false.
